edges.description: Mutant ferritin perturbs cellular iron storage and handling.
edges.source: Mutant ferritin light chain disrupts iron storage

edges.description: Iron dyshomeostasis promotes pathologic iron accumulation in the basal ganglia.
Iron dyshomeostasis also promotes reactive oxygen species generation.
Iron deposition in central neurons contributes to the progressive neurodegenerative phenotype.
Oxidative injury likely contributes to the progressive neurodegenerative phenotype.
Basal ganglia neurodegeneration commonly manifests as dystonia.
Progressive basal ganglia injury commonly manifests as chorea.
More advanced basal ganglia injury can manifest as parkinsonism.

edges.target: Mutant ferritin light chain disrupts iron storage

nodes.description: Patient-derived fibroblasts show increased basal iron content and altered iron-regulatory machinery.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal iron metabolism in fibroblasts from a patient with the neurodegenerative disease hereditary ferritinopathy
evidence.snippet: Compared to normal controls, HF fibroblasts showed abnormal iron metabolism consisting of increased levels of ferritin polypeptides, divalent metal transporter 1, basal iron content and reactive oxygen species, and decreased levels of transferrin receptor-1 and IRE-IRP binding activity.

nodes.description: The combined effects of abnormal iron deposition and oxidative injury drive a progressive basal-ganglia movement disorder with adult onset and relentless progression.

evidence.snippet: Neuroferritinopathy (MIM 606159, also labeled hereditary ferritinopathy and neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation type 2, NBIA2) is an adult-onset progressive movement disorder caused by mutations in the ferritin light chain gene (FTL1).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and natural history of neuroferritinopathy caused by the FTL1 460InsA mutation.
evidence.snippet: The disease progressed relentlessly, becoming generalized over a 5-10 year period, eventually leading to aphonia, dysphagia and severe motor disability with subcortical/frontal cognitive dysfunction as a late feature.

nodes.description: Chorea is the most common presenting movement disorder, occurring in approximately 50% of patients at onset.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroferritinopathy: Pathophysiology, Presentation, Differential Diagnoses and Management
evidence.snippet: Chorea and dystonia are the most common presenting symptoms in NF.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and natural history of neuroferritinopathy caused by the FTL1 460InsA mutation.
evidence.snippet: The mean age of onset was 39.4 years (SD = 13.3, range 13-63), beginning with chorea in 50%, focal lower limb dystonia in 42.5% and parkinsonism in 7.5%.

nodes.description: Dystonia is one of the common presenting movement-disorder phenotypes, with focal lower limb dystonia as a presenting feature in approximately 42.5% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroferritinopathy: Pathophysiology, Presentation, Differential Diagnoses and Management
evidence.snippet: Chorea and dystonia are the most common presenting symptoms in NF.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and natural history of neuroferritinopathy caused by the FTL1 460InsA mutation.
evidence.snippet: The mean age of onset was 39.4 years (SD = 13.3, range 13-63), beginning with chorea in 50%, focal lower limb dystonia in 42.5% and parkinsonism in 7.5%.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal iron metabolism in fibroblasts from a patient with the neurodegenerative disease hereditary ferritinopathy
evidence.snippet: Nucleotide duplications in exon 4 of the ferritin light polypeptide (FTL) gene cause the autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease neuroferritinopathy or hereditary ferritinopathy (HF).

nodes.description: Abnormal iron metabolism increases reactive oxygen species and oxidative injury, contributing to ferritin aggregation and cellular damage.

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal iron metabolism in fibroblasts from a patient with the neurodegenerative disease hereditary ferritinopathy
evidence.snippet: Compared to normal controls, HF fibroblasts showed abnormal iron metabolism consisting of increased levels of ferritin polypeptides, divalent metal transporter 1, basal iron content and reactive oxygen species, and decreased levels of transferrin receptor-1 and IRE-IRP binding activity.

This structural alteration causes attenuated net iron incorporation leading to cellular iron mishandling, ferritin aggregation, and oxidative damage at physiological concentrations of iron and ascorbate.

nodes.description: Abnormal ferritin and iron accumulation occur predominantly in the basal ganglia, with cystic cavitation as a hallmark finding. Cerebellar atrophy is also observed.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroferritinopathy: Pathophysiology, Presentation, Differential Diagnoses and Management
evidence.snippet: Background: Neuroferritinopathy (NF) is a rare autosomal dominant disease caused by mutations in the ferritin light chain 1 (FTL1) gene leading to abnormal excessive iron accumulation in the brain, predominantly in the basal ganglia.
The main pathologic findings are cystic cavitation of the basal ganglia, the presence of ferritin inclusion bodies (IBs), and substantial iron deposition.

evidence.reference_title: Voxel-based analysis in neuroferritinopathy expands the phenotype and determines radiological correlates of disease severity.
evidence.snippet: Our data show that progressive iron accumulation in the caudate nucleus, and cavitation of the globus pallidus correlate with disease severity in neuroferritinopathy. We also confirm sub-clinical cerebellar atrophy as a feature of the disease.

nodes.description: Pathogenic FTL variants alter ferritin structure and impair normal cellular iron handling.
nodes.id: Mutant ferritin light chain disrupts iron storage

evidence.reference_title: Abnormal iron metabolism in fibroblasts from a patient with the neurodegenerative disease hereditary ferritinopathy
evidence.snippet: Nucleotide duplications in exon 4 of the ferritin light polypeptide (FTL) gene cause the autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease neuroferritinopathy or hereditary ferritinopathy (HF).

nodes.description: Some patients develop parkinsonian features as part of the progressive basal-ganglia disorder, though isolated parkinsonism is unusual.

evidence.snippet: A wide range of neurologic symptoms may occur; 50% present with chorea, 43% with limb dystonia, and 7% with Parkinsonian features.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features and natural history of neuroferritinopathy caused by the FTL1 460InsA mutation.
evidence.snippet: In conclusion, isolated parkinsonism is unusual in neuroferritinopathy, and unlike Huntington's disease, cognitive changes are absent or subtle in the early stages.
